items.myth: k-means always finds the globally best clustering.
items.reality: k-means minimizes WCSS via Lloyd's algorithm but only reaches a local minimum. Different initial centroids can yield different partitions with different inertia.
items.example: Two runs on the same data with different `random_state` values may assign borderline points to different clusters even when both converge.

items.myth: The centroid must be an actual data point.
items.reality: Centroids are mean vectors and often lie between points—not necessarily equal to any single observation.
items.example: Clustering points (0,0) and (2,2) yields centroid (1,1), which is not in the original dataset.

items.myth: Increasing k always produces better, more useful clusters.
items.reality: WCSS always decreases as k approaches n, but extra clusters may over-split noise and harm interpretability. Utility depends on validation, not inertia alone.
items.example: At k=n each point is its own cluster with zero WCSS—a mathematically perfect but operationally useless partition.
